coredata.dc:title: Slow release performance of the immobilized carbon dots and their enhancing effect on anaerobic denitrification
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Fanghao
Li, Qiansheng
Lu, Hong
Li, Xiaoman
Zhou, Jiti
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) can improve microbial electron transfer rates and denitrification activities via entering the cells. To avoid the frequent addition of CDs in practical application, in this study, CDs were first covalently immobilized on bio-carrier polypropylene nonwoven fabrics. The immobilized CDs (iCDs) could be slowly released and enter the cells, thus enhancing the activities of the denitrifying-related enzymes and electron transfer system of Stutzerimonas stutzeri ATCC 17588, during which the nitrate removal rate was significantly increased by 47.2 % with the reduced accumulation of nitrite and N2O. During the tested 10 cycles, the iCDs exhibited stable accelerating performances on nitrate removal (an average increase of 39.9 %). Proteomics analysis showed that the slow-release performance of iCDs was mainly attributed to the catalytic effect of extracellular amidases and esterases. At the community level, the iCDs addition also resulted in the increase of denitrification rate mediated by the enriched denitrification community containing main genera Stutzerimonas, Thauera and Pannonibacter. Further metagenomics analysis showed that the three genera containing genes encoding amidases and denitrifying-related enzymes could perform denitrification process together via obtaining iCDs. These results indicate that iCDs are of potential application for the treatment of nitrate-containing wastewaters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Slow release
Immobilized carbon dots
Anaerobic denitrification
Amidases and esterases
Bacterial community

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Batch experiments of iCDs-mediated microbial denitrification
Slow-release performance experiments of iCDs mediated by strain ATCC 17588
Analysis of extracellular proteins of strain ATCC 17558 and metagenomics of the enriched denitrification community
Effects of iCDs on denitrification by strain ATCC 17588
Slow-release performance and mechanism of iCDs
Effect of iRCDs on denitrification by the enriched denitrification community

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs with inset of the size distribution of CDs. (b) HR-TEM of CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum. (d) Enlarged FT-IR spectrum. (e) N1s of XPS spectrum. (f) O1s of XPS spectrum. (g-i) SEM elemental mapping of iCDs.

Effects of iCDs on the denitrification rate by strain ATCC 17588. (a, b) Nitrate removal and nitrite accumulation in the presence of various materials. (c) Effect of iCDs on cell growth. (d) Effects of iCDs on nitrogen transformation.(e) Effects of iCDs on LDH release, the concentrations of NADH and ATP, the activities of ETSA and complex Ⅲ. (f) Effects of iCDs on the activities of denitrification enzymes. (* p≤0.05, ** p≤0.01, *** p≤0.001).

(a, b) Effect of iCDs with different qualities of CDs on nitrate removal and nitrite accumulation. (c) Rate of nitrate removal in ten repeated experiments. (d) Effect of different systems on the release of iCDs. (e) Effect of iCDs on the induction of extracellular proteins from strain ATCC 17588. (f) Effect of extracellular proteins on the release of iCDs.

Effect of iCDs on nitrate reduction by the enriched denitrification community. (a) Nitrate removal, (b) Nitrite accumulation. (c) Effects of iCDs on nitrogen transformation. (d) Effect of iCDs on the induction of extracellular proteins from bacterial community. (e) Effects of iCDs on ETSA of bacterial community. (f) Effect of iCDs on the structure of bacterial community at the genus levels.
Effect of iCDs on the abundance of various genes coding TCA-related enzymes. The relative abundance of genes (%) was amplified by 1000 folds.
Effect of iCDs on the abundance of genes encoding key enzymes. (a) ATP. (b) Complex Ⅰ. (c) Complex Ⅲ. (d) Denitrification and amidase. The relative abundance of genes (%) was amplified by 1000 folds.
Amidases and esterases detected by Label-Free proteomics.

head.ce:title: Slow release performance of the immobilized carbon dots and their enhancing effect on anaerobic denitrification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, China, 116024

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) can improve microbial electron transfer rates and denitrification activities via entering the cells. To avoid the frequent addition of CDs in practical application, in this study, CDs were first covalently immobilized on bio-carrier polypropylene nonwoven fabrics. The immobilized CDs (iCDs) could be slowly released and enter the cells, thus enhancing the activities of the denitrifying-related enzymes and electron transfer system of Stutzerimonas stutzeri ATCC 17588, during which the nitrate removal rate was significantly increased by 47.2 % with the reduced accumulation of nitrite and N2O. During the tested 10 cycles, the iCDs exhibited stable accelerating performances on nitrate removal (an average increase of 39.9 %). Proteomics analysis showed that the slow-release performance of iCDs was mainly attributed to the catalytic effect of extracellular amidases and esterases. At the community level, the iCDs addition also resulted in the increase of denitrification rate mediated by the enriched denitrification community containing main genera Stutzerimonas, Thauera and Pannonibacter. Further metagenomics analysis showed that the three genera containing genes encoding amidases and denitrifying-related enzymes could perform denitrification process together via obtaining iCDs. These results indicate that iCDs are of potential application for the treatment of nitrate-containing wastewaters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The discharge of wastewaters containing excessive nitrate can give rise to severe environmental challenges including the eutrophication of rivers and lakes, a decline in aquatic biodiversity and the deterioration of underground water, ultimately posing a threat to human health [1–3]. Hence, the elimination of nitrate in wastewaters has received much attention. Bio-denitrification technology is widely used for nitrate removal [4,5]. However, anaerobic bio-denitrification rate is low due to the slow rate of microbial electron transfer.

Some carbon-based materials including bulk biochar, graphene oxide, graphite and carbon dots (CDs) have been reported to be used as electron mediators for enhancing anaerobic bio-denitrification rate [6–9]. Especially, CDs can directly accelerate intracellular electron transfer via entering cells during bio-denitrification process [10]. However, CDs are easily discharged with the effluent due to their high solubility. To avoid the frequent addition of CDs in practical application, CDs may be immobilized on biofilm carriers. If bacterial cells can obtain the immobilized CDs via the catalysis of the secreted enzymes, the released CDs could enter bacterial cells to increase the denitrification rate. It was reported that the denitrification strains are generally capable of synthesizing and secreting amidases and esterases, which are responsible for the cleavage of amide and ester bonds, respectively [11–13]. Thus, the two kinds of bonds are suggested to be used for CDs immobilization. Previous studies mainly focused on the immobilization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for enhancing water permeability and anti-organic fouling potential of membranes [14]. During this process, there was only the occurrence of the amidation reaction between carboxylated GQDs and aminated membranes. Moreover, the bioavailability of the immobilized CDs was unclear in biosystems.

Polymer-based non-woven fabrics have been used as biocarriers for wastewater treatment due to their good biocompatibility, low cost and high mechanical strength. In the present study, polypropylene non-woven fabric was used as a biocarrier, CDs were first immobilized on the surface of carboxylated fabrics via amidation and esterification reactions. Considering that Stutzerimonas genus is usually the dominant species in the denitrification process of municipal wastewaters [15], Stutzerimonas stutzeri ATCC 17588 was used as a model strain. Meanwhile, a bacterial community capable of denitrification was also enriched. The accelerating effects of the immobilized CDs on anaerobic bio-denitrification using strain ATCC 17588 and the enriched community were investigated. Moreover, the slow-release mechanism of the immobilized CDs was revealed using proteomics. The enriched denitrification community was also analyzed using metagenomics.

Stutzerimonas stutzeri ATCC 17588 was purchased from the China Agricultural Culture Collection and can be found in GenBank with the accession number CP002881. The enrichment process of a denitrification community and the used medium were described in the Supporting Materials (Text S1).

Luria-Bertani (LB) medium was composed of 10 g/L NaCl, 10 g/L tryptone, and 5 g/L yeast extract. The used minimal medium (MM, pH 7.5) consisted of: 40 mM CH3COONa, 2.88 g/L Na2HPO4, 10 mM NaNO3, 0.48 g/L KH2PO4, 0.02 g/L CaCl2, and 0.02 g/L MgSO4. The details of the reagents were shown in the Supplementary Materials (Text S2).

CDs were prepared using a microwave digestion method. Briefly, 0.2 g of citric acid (CA) and 300 µL of diethylenetriamine (DETA) were added into 5 mL of ultrapure water. Microwave digestion was performed in 3 minutes. After the reaction was completed, a reddish-brown solid was obtained. Then it was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane, and purified in a 1 KD dialysis bag for 24 h. Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried to obtain CDs for subsequent experiments.

The prepared CDs were immobilized using a chemical method. Firstly, carboxylated fabrics were prepared through the following three steps: (1) A 2 % Fe(NH4)2·(SO4)2·6 H2O aqueous solution and a 1 % benzophenone (Ph2CO) ethanol solution were mixed in a volume ratio of 5:1, and then acrylic acid (AA) was added into the above solution (v/v=3:7) to obtain a UV grafting solution. (2) New fabrics were pre-soaked in acetone for 12 h, then washed with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water, and dried. (3) These fabrics with the sizes of 5 × 5 cm were put into an anaerobic transparent bottle containing 30 mL of UV grafting solution. After flushing with N2 for 15 min, the bottle was placed in the dark for 12 h, and then irradiated using a 20 W/365 nm UV lamp for 1 h, with a light source distance of 1 cm from these fabrics. After the reaction was completed, the treated fabrics were ultrasonically washed with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water for 30 min, and dried in a 70 ℃ oven as carboxylated fabrics. Secondly, CDs (20–80 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and reacted with 0.3 g of carboxylated fabrics in an oil bath at 80 ℃ for 8 h. The obtained CDs-modified fabrics were named as the immobilized CDs (iCDs). Based on the initial concentration of the used CDs in the above reactions, these prepared iCDs were named as iCDs-20, iCDs-40, iCDs-60 and iCDs-80, respectively. If not specified, the iCDs in the following pages all represented iCDs-80.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Batch experiments of iCDs-mediated microbial denitrification
ce:section.ce:para: Strain ATCC 17588 was aerobically cultured in LB medium at 35 °C with 150 rpm for 12 h, and then the cells were harvested by centrifugation (9000 g, 4 min). The harvested cells were washed three times with saline solution (0.9 % NaCl) and resuspended in the same solution for further usage. 100 mL of MM (pH 7.5) was added into each 110 mL serum bottle. These bottles were purged with N2/He for 15 minutes and then sealed. After being autoclaved at 121°C for 20 minutes, initial fabrics, carboxylated fabrics and CDs-modified fabrics with equal sizes of 5×5 cm were added into serum bottles in an anaerobic incubator. Subsequently, strain ATCC 17588 (OD600=0.1)/the enriched denitrification community (Protein content=14 mg/L) was inoculated into these serum bottles. Then these bottles were incubated in an incubator at 35 °C with 150 rpm. The effect of iCDs concentration was also tested. Samples were taken out using syringes at certain intervals under anaerobic conditions and centrifuged with 10 000 g for 5 min. The effect of the obtained supernatants on nitrate bio-removal was also studied. The released CDs and extracellular proteins in the supernatants were tested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Slow-release performance experiments of iCDs mediated by strain ATCC 17588
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared iCDs were immersed in MM, MM containing cells and MM containing the heat-killed cells for 12 h, respectively. Then the supernatants of the above systems were collected using centrifugation (10000 g, 10 min). The concentration of free CDs in the supernatants was detected at 363 nm by a UV–VIS spectrophotometry. The effect of extracellular supernatant on the release of iCDs was tested. Specifically, during strain ATCC 17588-mediated process without iCDs, the samples were taken every 2 h and centrifuged at 10 000 g for 5 min. Then the obtained supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. Subsequently, iCDs were immersed in the above supernatants for 2 h in an incubator at 35°C with 150 rpm and the concentration of the released CDs in the supernatants was quantified.

The effect of the prepared iCDs on nitrate removal was also tested in 10 cycles with strain ATCC 17588. Briefly, after each run, the iCDs were sonicated with anhydrous ethanol for 2 h and then with ultrapure water for 10 h. Subsequently, the iCDs were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 4 h and then used for the next run. After each run of 8 h, NO3 --N and NO2 --N concentrations were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of extracellular proteins of strain ATCC 17558 and metagenomics of the enriched denitrification community
ce:section.ce:para: Extracellular proteins of strain ATCC 17558-mediated system containing iCDs were analyzed. Detailed information on the label-free quantitative proteomics could be found in the Supplementary Materials (Text S3). Furthermore, the detailed information on the metagenomic analysis of the enriched denitrification community could be found in the Supplementary Materials (Text S4).

The particle size and structure of CDs were analyzed by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan). The surface morphologies of initial fabrics, carboxylated fabrics and CDs-modified fabrics were compared by using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, NOVA NanoSEM 450, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Escalab 250Xi, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo IS50, USA) were used to investigate their elemental composition and chemical groups, respectively. The concentrations of NO3 --N and NO2 --N were measured using the salicylic acid-concentrated sulfuric acid spectrophotometric method and the N-(1-naphthyl)-ethylenediamine dihydrochloride method, respectively [16,17]. The measurement of total nitrogen (TN) solution was as follows: samples including the supernatant and bacterial precipitation were firstly treated by alkaline persulfate digestion at the autoclave (121℃, 30 min), during which N in different forms could be all converted to NO3 -, and the concentration of NO3 - was tested. Nitrogen content of cells in solutions was calculated by deducting NO3 - and NO2 - from TN in the solution. The concentrations of N2O and N2 in the gas phase were measured by using a GC 7900 gas chromatography (Techcomp, China) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector and a TDX-01 column (2 m × 4 mm) under a carrier gas of He. The temperatures of the oven and detector were 60 ℃ and 120 ℃, respectively. The corresponding dissolved gas concentration was calculated based on Henry's Law [18]. The protein content of cells was measured with a Bradford assay kit (Beyotime, China). Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release was measured by using a cytotoxicity detection kit (Leagene, China). NADH and ATP were determined using the NAD+/NADH assay kit (Beyotime, China) and microbial cell activity assay kit (BacTiter Glo, Germany), respectively. Additional details of the methods for ETSA assays and enzyme activity assays can be found in the Supplementary Materials (Text S5 and Text S6).

All data were obtained in triplicate and presented as mean ± standard deviation. GraphPad Prism 8 with a two-tailed t-test was used to analyze the difference, and a p-value less than 0.05 represents the significance. (ns p>0.05, * p≤0.05, ** p≤0.01, *** p≤0.001).

CDs were first prepared and shown in Fig. 1. TEM image analysis showed that the prepared CDs with an average diameter of 3 nm had a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm ( Fig. 2a, b), which corresponded to the (100) plane of graphite [19]. This structure indicated that CDs were of good crystalline. Furthermore, the optical properties of CDs were analyzed. The typical UV–VIS absorption peaks at 254 nm and 363 nm come from the structures of graphite CC (π-π* transition) and CO/CN (n-π* transition), respectively (Fig. S1a). CDs showed the maximum fluorescence intensity under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S1b). In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, the characteristic peaks observed at 3432 cm−1, 3272 cm−1 and 1657 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of -OH, -NH2 and CO, respectively (Fig. 2c). The presence of NH2- and OH- groups was beneficial for subsequent CDs immobilization.

Meanwhile, carboxylated fabrics as carriers were prepared via the ultraviolet grafting reaction (Fig. 1). The new peaks in the FT-IR of carboxylated fabrics were observed at 1695 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1, which corresponded to the CO and C-OH stretching vibrations [20], respectively, compared with those in the FT-IR of initial fabrics (Fig. 2d). This result indicated that the carboxyl group was successfully grafted on the surface of fabrics. SEM analysis showed that the surface of carboxylated fabrics was rougher than that of fabrics (Fig. S1d, e). After the prepared CDs were immobilized on carboxylated fabrics, the rough extent of its surface decreased, indicating that CDs effectively filled surface voids (Fig. S1e, f). SEM mapping also results demonstrated that O and N elements of CDs were successfully immobilized on the surface of fabrics (Fig. 2g-i and Fig. S1g-i). Meanwhile, Fig. S1c shows that the contact angle of iCDs was 0°, which was much lower than that of fabrics (125°). This result indicated that there was a transition from hydrophobic to hydrophilic characteristic, providing favourable conditions for their applications in biological systems.

Further FT-IR spectra analysis showed that the new absorption peaks in the spectrum of CDs-modified fabrics were observed at 1644 cm−1 and 1542 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO and the bending vibration of N-H in amide bonds, respectively, compared with those of carboxylated fabrics (Fig. 2c). Moreover, the two peaks displayed distinct rightward shifts compared with those in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. These shifts implied the formation of amide bonds. In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-modified fabrics (Fig. 2c), the intensities of the absorption peaks of the stretching vibration of CO and C-O-C in ester group were also increased at 1712 cm−1 and 1162 cm−1 compared with those in the spectrum of CDs, indicating the formation of ester bonds. These results showed that iCDs were successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of iCDs on denitrification by strain ATCC 17588
ce:section.ce:para: In bioreaction system, the addition of initial fabrics had little effect on nitrate removal and nitrite accumulation ( Fig. 3a). Different from initial fabrics, additional carboxylated fabrics inhibited nitrate removal, because the presence of multiple carboxyls inhibited the growth of cells. In comparison, iCDs addition increased cell growth rate (Fig. 3c). More importantly, iCDs remarkably enhanced nitrate reduction with 99.5 % removal rate by using strain ATCC 17588 in 8 h compared with that without iCDs (52.3 %). Moreover, nitrite accumulation in iCDs-amended system was only 0.33 mM in 10 h, while it in the bare cells (control) system reached 2.93 mM (Fig. 3b). NH4 + was not detected in the whole denitrification process. As an intermediate in denitrification, N2O tends to accumulate and cause acute environmental pollution owing to incomplete denitrification [21,22]. Therefore, nitrate transformation products in iCDs-amended system were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3d, compared with the N2O-N concentration in the control system (1.96 mM), its concentration was reduced to 1.22 mM at 8 h after the addition of iCDs. Furthermore, the N2-N concentration in iCDs-amended system (7.63 mM) was 10.7-fold higher at 8 h compared with that in the control group (0.71 mM). These results demonstrated that iCDs accelerated the transformation of nitrate to N2 and reduced the accumulation of N2O and NO2 -. LDH release is commonly used to assess cellular structural integrity [23]. Our analysis showed that the addition of iCDs resulted in 18.6 % reduction in LDH release (Fig. 3e), indicating that iCDs had good compatibility with strain ATCC 17588.

During denitrification process, NADH as an intracellular electron mediator plays an important role in cells [24]. ATP, as a molecule for energy storage and metabolism, provides a valuable metric for gauging the speed of carbon metabolism [25]. In iCDs-amended system, the contents of NADH and ATP were significantly increased by 37.9 % and 59.0 %, respectively, compared with those in the control system (Fig. 3e). Moreover, iCDs addition resulted in 29.7 % increase in the ETSA of the reaction system. In intracellular electron transfer chain, the activity of complex bc1 (complex III) was increased by 128.8 % in iCDs-amended system compared with the control group (Fig. 3e). The activities of the NAR, NIR, NOR, and N2OR were increased by 37.9 %, 9.6 %, 137.4 %, and 80.4 %, respectively, after the addition of iCDs (Fig. 3f). These findings suggested that iCDs could enhance NADH synthesis, energy production and the activities of the related enzymes, ultimately supporting the denitrification process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Slow-release performance and mechanism of iCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The iCDs with different grafting amounts of CDs were further prepared and their accelerating effects were compared ( Fig. 4a, b). The results revealed that the promotion effects of iCDs with different grafting amounts of CDs had little difference. At the end of the reaction, the concentrations of extracellular free CDs in the supernatants of the iCDs-20, iCDs-40, iCDs-60 and iCDs-80 amended systems were approaching 22.0±4.1 mg/L, indicating that free CDs was similar in 8 h of incubation when iCDs was prepared using over 20 mg CDs.

The effect of iCDs was further tested in 10 cycles. Fig. 4c shows that the nitrate removal efficiency was maintained at 84.3∼100 % and the accumulated amount of nitrite was only 0.05 mM∼0.54 mM at 8 h in 10 cycles (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, the concentration of extracellular free CDs in the supernatant reached around 23.3±1.9 mg/L in each run. These results indicated that the immobilized CDs could be slowly released to effectively enhance denitrification.

Further XPS analysis revealed that there was no significant change in XPS N1s and O 1 s between the released CDs (r-CDs) and initial CDs (Fig. 2e, f), indicating that the cleavage location of some amide/esters bonds was consistent with the location of the formed amide/ester bonds of CDs immobilization. It is well known that the cleavage of the two kinds of bonds is commonly influenced by a multitude of factors, including solution pH, temperature and the secreted enzymes [26–28]. In the present study, the supernatant was obtained when nitrate was completely removed in MM+cells-mediated system. Then new CDs-modified fabrics were immersed in the supernatant for 8 h. As shown in Fig. 4d, the concentration of free CDs in the supernatant of MM+cells-mediated system reached 51.4 mg/L. In comparison, the release amount of iCDs in the heat-killed supernatant of MM+cells-mediated system decreased to 6.3 mg/L, which was similar to that in the supernatant of MM-mediated system. These results indicated that MM had minor effect, and the proteins in the supernatant played an important role in the release of iCDs. Further study found that iCDs addition promoted the secretion of cellular proteins (Fig. 4e). Moreover, with the increase of extracellular protein concentration in the supernatants of cells-mediated system, the release amount of iCDs in the supernatants rose (Fig. 4 f). Thus, it was assumed that strain ATCC 17588 secreted amidases and esterases to cleave the corresponding bonds for the release of iCDs.

The extracellular proteins of strain ATCC 17588 in iCDs-amended system were analyzed using proteomics technique. The results showed that a total of 5196 proteins were identified. Among these proteins, there were two amidases and one esterase: N-carbamoylcarnitine amidase, intracellular proteases/amidases and arylesterase ( Table 1). As intracellular proteases/amidases were located in cells, it was assumed that N-carbamoylcarnitine amidase and arylesterase played important roles in the initial phase of iCDs release. With the death and lysis of cells, the three enzymes might act together on the cleavage of the amide and ester bonds of iCDs, but exactly how they do remains to be investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of iRCDs on denitrification by the enriched denitrification community
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the application potential of iCDs, the bacterial community capable of denitrification was enriched using sodium acetate as an electron donor. The effect of iCDs on nitrate removal by the denitrification community was also investigated ( Fig. 5). The results showed that nitrate removal percentage (97.8 %) increased by 29.4 % in 8 h in the presence of iCDs compared to that without iCDs (68.4 %). Meanwhile, nitrite and N2O-N in iCDs-amended system were completely removed in 10 h, while the accumulation concentrations of nitrite and N2O-N in the control system reached 0.4 mM and 2.67 mM at 10 h, respectively. Furthermore, the N2-N concentration in iCDs-amended system (7.71 mM) was 2.15-fold higher at 10 h compared with that in the control group (3.58 mM) (Fig. 5c). During the above process, NH4 + was not detected. The iCDs addition promoted the secretion of the proteins of the denitrification community, and the released concentration of iCDs in reaction system rose with the increased concentration of extracellular proteins (Fig. 5d). This behavior was similar to that in strain ATCC 17588-mediated system.

The denitrification community in reaction system was analyzed using metagenomics sequencing technique. The results showed that Stutzerimonas genus was dominant in the denitrification community, accounting for more than 49 % in the control and iCDs-amended systems (Fig. 5f). The populations of the other two main genera Thauera and Pannonibacter in the iCDs-amended system were increased by 8.3 % and 48.2 %, respectively, compared with those in the control system. It has been reported that the above species are widely present in nitrate-containing wastewater and can remove nitrate via denitrification [29].

It is well known that the TCA cycle is the main pathway for carbon metabolism. In the TCA cycle, the enzymes capable of generating NADH are malate dehydrogenase and isocitrate dehydrogenase [22]. As shown in Fig. 6, the relative abundance of genes encoding isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) and malate dehydrogenase (mqo) were increased by 8.75 % and 1.7 %, respectively, compared to those in the control group. This result suggests that iCDs can promote the enrichment of genes related to NADH generation. Fig. 6 shows that the relative abundance of genes encoding succinyl-CoA synthetase (sucC and sucD) was increased by 6.15 % compared to controls in the iCDs test. Furthermore, the relative abundances of genes encoding complex I (NADH dehydrogenase, nuoA to nuoN), complex Ⅱ (succinate dehydrogenase, sdhA to sdhD) and complex III (petA to petC) in iCDs-amended system were increased by 19.4 %,11.4 % and 24.8 %, respectively, compared with those in the control system (Fig. 6 and Fig. 7b, c). Meanwhile, the relative abundances of genes related to ATP synthesis (atpA to atpG) were also increased by 21.1 % after the addition of iCDs (Fig. 7a). The enrichment of the above genes was beneficial for increasing the electron transfer rate. Further analysis demonstrated that iCDs addition also resulted in 55.9 % increase in the ETSA of the denitrification community-mediated system (Fig. 5e). ATP content and the activities of complex Ⅰ and complex III in ICDs-amended system were increased by 7.7 %, 206.4 % and 62.5 % compared with those in the control system, respectively (Fig. S3).

The addition of iCDs also increased the total relative abundances of the genes encoding nitrate (narGHI), nitric oxide (norBC), and nitrous oxide (nosZ) reductases in Stutzerimonas (2.7 %) and Pannonibacter (37.2 %), respectively (Fig. 7d). For Thauera, the total relative abundance of napA encoding nitrate reductase and norBC in iCDs-amended system was increased by 24.9 % compared with those in the control system. For nirK gene encoding nitrite reductase, its relative abundance in Pannonibacter was observed with 19.9 % increase after the addition of iCDs. These results indicated that iCDs addition promoted the three genera to perform anaerobic denitrification together. The analysis of the expression products of the above genes showed that the addition of iCDs resulted in 57.3 %, 94.7 %, 296.1 %, and 108.3 % increases in the activites of NAR, NIR, NOR, and N2OR reductases, respectively (Fig. S4), further demonstrating that iCD accelerated denitrification process.

It was also found that the relative abundances of amiE gene encoding amidase in Thauera and Pannonibacter and aguB gene encoding N-carbamoylputrescine amidase in Stutzerimonas were increased by 22.6 % and 13.9 %, respectively, after the addition of iCDs (Fig. 7d). It is reported that AmiE with a wide substrate spectrum can cleave most amide bonds [30]. It is noteworthy that N-carbamoylputrescine amidase was also found in the extracellular supernatant of strain ATCC 17588. However, the enrichment of the genes encoding esterases was not observed. These results indicated that iCDs promoted the enrichment of the genes encoding amidases in the denitrification community, which secreted amidases to cleave the amide bonds of iCDs for obtaining the rCDs.

As a new type of electron shuttles, CDs have attracted increasing attention owing to their low toxicity, easy synthesis and low cost. To better realize their application, CDs immobilization was performed in the present study. Our studies showed that, for pure culture Stutzerimonas strain and the enriched denitrification community, they could obtain CDs from iCDs via secreting amidases or esterases. Meanwhile, it was observed that amidases were the main enzymes responsible for the release of the iCDs. This behavior of iCDs was beneficial for persistently accelerating microbial denitrification. Our results also demonstrated that the addition of iCDs could significantly accelerate the denitrification and reduce the accumulation of nitrite and N2O by increasing the activities of enzymes and the enrichment of genes involved in denitrification process. For iCDs application, it is possible that biofilm will be cultured on CDs-modified carriers so that the iCDs can be released in biofilm and the partial free CDs enter cells, which was beneficial for accelerating the enrichment of functional bacterial community and the start-up of the reaction system. Additionally, recent studies showed that CDs could also promote the removal of other pollutants such as sulfamethoxazole, aniline and hexavalent chromium [31–33], and iCDs preparation are of potential application for accelerating the removal of these pollutants. However, iCDs also exist shortcomings such as effect deterioration during long-term tests and low grafting of CDs. Subsequently, new methods for CDs immobilization will be investigated to increase the amount of grafting.

In this study, iCDs were prepared by using a covalent binding method. The iCDs could release free CDs to enhance the denitrification efficiency by using strain ATCC 17588 and the enriched denitrification community, which was attributed to the enhanced activities of denitrifying-related enzymes and electron transport system. The slow-release effect could be driven by extracellular amidases/esterases. Even in the tested 10 cycles, iCDs still kept a high accelerating effect on nitrate removal, during which little nitrite was accumulated. This work provided a new strategy for CDs application during nitrate treatment.

Xiaoman Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiti Zhou: Supervision, Resources. Qiansheng Li: Writing – original draft, Software. Hong Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Fanghao Cheng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.114947.
